source.title: High-throughput screening of spike variants uncovers the key residues that alter the affinity and antigenicity of SARS-CoV-2

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to systematically identify key residues in the full-length SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) protein that alter ACE2-binding affinity and monoclonal antibody (mAb) evasion. The motivation is the rapid evolution of S, which affects binding and immune escape, making it difficult to predict the next dominant variant. Existing deep mutational scanning (DMS) studies were limited to the RBD in yeast, which has different glycosylation patterns from human cells, and did not cover the full S protein. The authors hypothesize that a mammalian cell surface-display platform can better mimic authentic viral infection and reveal crucial residues influencing affinity and antigenicity. They aim to create a comprehensive mutational landscape to understand key residues and enable monitoring of potential risks.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: The study established a mammalian cell-surface-display platform to study S-ACE2 and S-mAb interactions at scale. A lentivirus library of S variants was generated via in silico chip synthesis and site-directed saturation mutagenesis. Enriched candidates were obtained by single-cell fluorescence sorting and analyzed by third-generation sequencing. The mutational landscape revealed key residues affecting ACE2 binding and mAb evasion. Specific mutations (S205F, Y453F, Q493A, Q493M, Q498H, Q498Y, N501F, N501T) showed 3–12-fold increase in infectivity, with Y453F, Q493A, Q498Y exhibiting ≥10-fold resistance to mAbs REGN10933, LY-CoV555, REGN10987, respectively. The methods may assist in future control of SARS-CoV-2.
process_signals: Established a robust mammalian cell-surface-display platform
Generated lentivirus library via in silico chip synthesis and site-directed saturation mutagenesis
Enriched candidates through single-cell fluorescence sorting
Analyzed by third-generation DNA sequencing
Identified specific mutations with increased infectivity and mAb resistance
methods: Lentivirus library generation via in silico chip synthesis

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: SARS-CoV-2 S protein binds ACE2 to enter cells. Five VOCs and eight VOIs have emerged, with Delta and Omicron being highly transmissible. The S protein is heavily glycosylated, and prior DMS studies in yeast are limited because yeast glycosylation differs from human cells and only the RBD was studied. The authors aim to use a mammalian cell surface-display of intact S variants to study ACE2 binding and mAb evasion in a high-throughput manner, to reveal crucial residues and enable monitoring of potential risks.
process_signals: Knowledge gap: yeast DMS limited to RBD, different glycosylation
Hypothesis: mammalian display better mimics authentic environment
Goal: reveal crucial residues of full-length S variants influencing ACE2 binding and mAb evasion
failures_or_limits: Prior DMS in yeast does not reflect human glycosylation
Majority of S protein not yet investigated

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Establishing a mammalian cell assay for surface-displaying S variants
section_gists.gist: The parental WT S protein (Wuhan-Hu-1) was truncated at C-terminal 19 aa and fused to mCherry via T2A in a lentivirus backbone. HEK293T cells stably expressing WT-S showed no ACE2 dose-dependent binding, likely due to furin cleavage. Mutating the furin cleavage site (682-RRAR-685 to GSAS) and stabilizing the prefusion state (986-KV-987 to PP) created Δfurin-S, which showed ACE2 dose-dependent binding. This Δfurin-S was used as the template for the library.
process_signals: Initial failure: WT-S cell line showed no ACE2 dose-dependent binding
Hypothesis: S protein cleaved by furin-like proteases, losing binding
Solution: mutated furin cleavage site and added stabilizing proline mutations (Δfurin-S)
Success: Δfurin-S cell line showed ACE2 dose-dependent binding
Decision: use Δfurin-S as template for library
methods: Lentivirus backbone with S variant fused to mCherry via T2A
Stable cell line generation in HEK293T
failures_or_limits: WT-S cell line did not show ACE2 binding due to furin cleavage

section_gists.heading: High-throughput screening of S variants for increased ACE2-binding affinity and mAb evasion
section_gists.gist: Saturation mutagenesis was performed on the 1254 aa Δfurin-S, replacing each position (except Met1) with 19 other amino acids, totaling 23,807 variants. Oligo pools were synthesized on a chip and cloned into the lentivirus plasmid. The library was packaged into lentivirus and transduced into HEK293T cells at low MOI to ensure one variant per cell. Cells were sorted by FACS: first screen selected cells with high ACE2 binding (top ~10% FITC/mCherry ratio), second screen selected cells resistant to mAb REGN10987 (top ~1-2% FITC/mCherry ratio in presence of mAb). Sorted cells were sequenced by PacBio SMRT. Enrichment was calculated as fold change (FC) of mutation frequency in sorted vs. unsorted cells.
process_signals: Design: saturation mutagenesis of full-length S (23,807 variants)
Library construction: chip-synthesized oligos, cloned into lentivirus plasmid
Transduction at low MOI for single-variant per cell
Two-step FACS: 1st for ACE2 binding, 2nd for mAb evasion
Sequencing: PacBio SMRT to link mutations
Enrichment metric: FC = frequency in sorted / frequency in unsorted

section_gists.heading: An overview of the mutational effect on affinity and antigenicity
section_gists.gist: For proof of concept, 40 sites were selected for validation. Thresholds: FC > 2 for enrichment, FC < 0.5 for de-enrichment. Of 23,807 mutations, 51.3% were detrimental, 36.8% neutral, 8.1% beneficial for ACE2 binding. Known VOC mutations (e.g., N501Y, E484K) were enriched. Average mutations per clone: ~2.3 in S_I, ~1.8 in S_II, ~2.4 in S_III, suggesting selection for fewer mutations in ACE2 binding but more in mAb evasion. Ten key RBD positions for affinity: V382, S383, T385, K386, L390, T430, S477, Q493, Q498, N501. For antigenicity, 13.4% beneficial for mAb resistance; ten key residues: S383, K386, L390, N439, N440, K444, V445, Q493, Q498, N501. Validation of 20 candidates with FC > 2 showed 19 with higher affinity than Δfurin-S.
process_signals: Validation of screening hits using selected sites
Observation: average mutations per clone changed across sorting stages, indicating selection pressures
Identification of key residues for affinity and antigenicity
Validation of top candidates confirmed increased affinity
methods: Bind score (average FC of all mutations at a residue)
Validation by ACE2 incubation assay (MFI measurement)
failures_or_limits: Some known affinity-decreasing mutations (K417T/N) not enriched

section_gists.heading: N-glycosylation plays a critical role in affinity
section_gists.gist: To explain why single mutations showed epistatic effects in multi-mutant variants, structural analysis suggested nearby disulfide bonds might stabilize prefusion state. Additionally, N-glycosylation was investigated. Mutants Y369N, A372S/T, L390S/T, G413N created new N-glycan motifs (N-X-S/T, X≠P). Ablation of natural N-glycan sites decreased affinity. Manually added N-glycan sites increased affinity. Glycosylation was confirmed by PNGase F treatment and SDS-PAGE shift. SPR showed increased affinity for these mutants.
process_signals: Hypothesis: epistatic effects due to disulfide bond stabilization or glycosylation
Observation: new N-glycan motifs created by specific mutations
Validation: natural N-glycan ablation decreases affinity; added N-glycans increase affinity
Confirmation of glycosylation by PNGase F and SPR
failures_or_limits: Incomplete glycosylation for L390S and G413N (mixture of 2 and 3 N-glycans)

section_gists.heading: Manually adding N-glycans strengthens affinity but attenuates infectivity
section_gists.gist: Four N-glycan sites (Y369N, A372T, L390S, G413N) were added to Delta and Omicron S proteins. L390S and G413N improved affinity in vitro (1.37-1.50 fold) and increased resistance to REGN10987. However, in a VSV-based pseudovirus system, these mutants drastically lost infectivity (e.g., L390S reduced infectivity ~816-fold in WT context). Similar loss observed for other N-glycan mutants. The mechanism was unclear, but findings emphasize N-glycan sites are critical for infectivity.
process_signals: Hypothesis: heavier glycosylation might increase infectivity
Test: added N-glycans to Delta and Omicron
Result: increased affinity and mAb resistance in vitro
Unexpected result: pseudovirus infectivity greatly reduced
Conclusion: N-glycans strengthen affinity but attenuate infectivity
methods: Site-directed mutagenesis to add N-glycan sites
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of infectivity attenuation not clear
Pseudovirus system may not fully recapitulate authentic virus

section_gists.heading: Identifying potentially high-risk mutation sites
section_gists.gist: 30 pseudoviruses were tested in 293T-ACE2-Furin cells. Four N-glycan mutants (Y369N, L390S, L390T, G413N) drastically lost infectivity; A372S/T slightly reduced. Nine mutations from F374 to N440 decreased infectivity 10-10,000 fold. T430Q, Q498W, N501Y maintained WT infectivity. Non-RBD mutations M900V and T1117M reduced infectivity. Eight variants (S205F, Y453F, Q493A, Q493M, Q498H, Q498Y, N501F, N501T) showed 3-12-fold increased infectivity over WT, surpassing D614G. No linear correlation between affinity and infectivity. S2/S ratio correlated with infectivity, suggesting furin cleavage efficiency affects entry. These eight mutants also showed resistance to REGN10987 and convalescent sera.
process_signals: Systematic testing of 30 pseudoviruses
Identification of high-risk mutations with increased infectivity
Observation: no linear correlation between affinity and infectivity
Hypothesis: S2/S ratio (furin cleavage) predicts infectivity
Validation: high-risk mutants show mAb and serum resistance
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of infectivity changes not fully explained

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study provides a comprehensive mutational profile of full-length S in mammalian cells. A small fraction of mutations enhance affinity (~8%) or mAb resistance (~13%). Key RBD residues for affinity and evasion were identified. Non-RBD mutations were mostly neutral or detrimental, except S205F. Manually added N-glycans increased affinity but reduced infectivity, suggesting potential as low-toxic antigens. The study correlates with GISAID mutation frequencies and notes epistatic and convergent evolution. Limitations include the complexity of multi-mutant effects and the need for further screening of Omicron. A similar mammalian DMS study was published during manuscript preparation.
process_signals: Summary of key findings
Comparison with yeast DMS: good correlation but new sites identified
Observation: N-glycan mutants as potential low-toxic antigens
Acknowledgment of epistatic and convergent evolution
Limitation: multi-mutant effects complex, next variant hard to predict
Note: similar mammalian DMS published during preparation
failures_or_limits: Complex recombinational effects of multiple sites
Next variant still hard to predict
Similar study published during manuscript preparation

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content provided.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Mammalian cell surface-display of full-length S protein better mimics authentic viral infection than yeast DMS of RBD alone.
Saturation mutagenesis of the entire S protein can reveal key residues for ACE2 affinity and mAb evasion that are missed by RBD-only studies.
Furin cleavage site mutation and prefusion stabilization are necessary for proper S display and ACE2 binding.
N-glycosylation plays a critical role in modulating ACE2 affinity and infectivity, with added N-glycans increasing affinity but attenuating infectivity.
High-throughput screening can identify high-risk mutations with increased infectivity and immune evasion potential.
Epistatic effects and multi-mutant combinations are important for viral evolution, but single mutations can have large effects.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the mechanism by which added N-glycans increase affinity but reduce infectivity?
How do the identified key residues interact in the context of multiple mutations (epistasis)?
Can the screening platform be applied to predict future variants of concern?
What are the limitations of using HEK293T cells and pseudovirus systems compared to authentic virus?
How generalizable are the findings to other mAbs and emerging variants?
What is the role of non-RBD mutations in viral fitness and immune evasion?

events.summary: Prior DMS studies limited to RBD in yeast with different glycosylation; majority of S protein not investigated.
Mammalian cell surface-display of intact S variants better mimics authentic viral infection for studying ACE2 binding and mAb evasion.
Designed lentivirus backbone with S variant fused to mCherry via T2A for surface display.
WT-S cell line showed no ACE2 dose-dependent binding, likely due to furin cleavage.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, problem statement, platform development, and screening design.

events.summary: Sequenced sorted cells by PacBio SMRT and calculated fold change (FC) of mutation frequency.
Classified mutational effects: 51.3% detrimental, 36.8% neutral, 8.1% beneficial for ACE2 binding.
Identified ten key RBD residues for ACE2 affinity: V382, S383, T385, K386, L390, T430, S477, Q493, Q498, N501.
Validated 20 candidates with FC > 2; 19 showed higher affinity than Δfurin-S, 8 reached VOC level.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers library design, screening, sequencing, and initial analysis of mutational landscape.

events.summary: PNGase F treatment confirmed glycosylation of RBD variants with manually added N-glycan sites.
numbers_or_metrics: MW: 39 kDa (3 N-glycans), 35 kDa (2 N-glycans), 31 kDa (1 N-glycan), 27 kDa (deglycosylated)

events.summary: N-glycan mutants added to Delta and Omicron S proteins increased affinity and mAb resistance in vitro.
numbers_or_metrics: L390S and G413N improved affinity 1.37–1.50-fold for Delta, 1.28–1.37-fold for Omicron

events.summary: Pseudovirus infectivity was drastically reduced for N-glycan mutants despite increased affinity.
numbers_or_metrics: L390S reduced infectivity ~816-fold in WT context

events.summary: Systematic pseudovirus testing identified eight mutations with 3–12-fold increased infectivity over WT.
numbers_or_metrics: 3–12-fold increase in infectivity compared to WT

events.summary: No linear correlation between affinity and infectivity; S2/S ratio correlated with infectivity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental validation of N-glycan effects, pseudovirus infectivity tests, and identification of high-risk mutations.

events.summary: Analysis of furin cleavage and infectivity: mutants with higher S2/S ratio or more sensitive furin-cleavage activity displayed higher infectivity; mutants with lower furin-cleavage activity reduced infectivity; mutants with inactivated furin-cleavage capacity largely attenuated infectivity.

Neutralization assay of high-risk mutants against commercial mAbs: Q493A and Q493M showed higher resistance to LY-CoV555; Y453F and Q493A prevented neutralization of REGN10933; Q498Y and Q498H exhibited evasion of REGN10987 and VIR-7831.
Serum neutralization assay: Q498Y exhibited broader defense against serum antibodies.
Library design: oligo pools (1254 × 19 = 23,807) used to mutate full-length S variants at specific sites via site-directed saturation mutagenesis to mimic random viral mutations.
Library quality control: optimized dosage, PCR parameters to generate high-coverage lentivirus library containing >96% of point mutations.
Limitation: recombinational effect of multiple sites is complex; next variant with survival advantage hard to predict; further screening of Omicron needed.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of furin cleavage and infectivity, neutralization data, library design details, summary of mutational effects, limitations, and comparison with other methods.

events.summary: Transduced HEK293T cells at low MOI to ensure single lentivirus entry per cell.
Sequenced sorted cell populations by PacBio SMRT and calculated fold change (FC) of mutation frequencies.
numbers_or_metrics: FC = proportion in S_II/S_I or S_III/S_I

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for library construction, lentivirus production, cell sorting, and sequencing, which are core to the high-throughput screening process.
This chunk contains only acknowledgments, author details, contributions, data availability, conflict of interest, publisher's note, and references. No research evolution events are described.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Systematically identify key residues in full-length SARS-CoV-2 spike protein that alter ACE2-binding affinity and monoclonal antibody evasion using a mammalian cell surface-display platform.

canonical_events.summary: Prior DMS studies limited to RBD in yeast with different glycosylation; majority of S protein not investigated.
Mammalian cell surface-display of intact S variants better mimics authentic viral infection for studying ACE2 binding and mAb evasion.
Designed lentivirus backbone with S variant fused to mCherry via T2A for surface display.
WT-S cell line showed no ACE2 dose-dependent binding, likely due to furin cleavage.
Mutated furin cleavage site (682-RRAR-685 to GSAS) and added prefusion-stabilizing proline mutations (986-KV-987 to PP) to create Δfurin-S, which showed ACE2 dose-dependent binding.
Library design: site-directed saturation mutagenesis of full-length S (1254 positions × 19 substitutions = 23,807 variants) using chip-synthesized oligo pools.
Library quality control: optimized PCR parameters to generate high-coverage lentivirus library containing >96% of point mutations.
Transduced HEK293T cells at low MOI (~0.1) to ensure single lentivirus entry per cell.
Sequenced sorted cells by PacBio SMRT and calculated fold change (FC) of mutation frequency (FC = proportion in sorted/unsorted).

Classified mutational effects: 51.3% detrimental, 36.8% neutral, 8.1% beneficial for ACE2 binding; identified ten key RBD residues for affinity (V382, S383, T385, K386, L390, T430, S477, Q493, Q498, N501).
Validated 20 candidates with FC > 2; 19 showed higher affinity than Δfurin-S, 8 reached VOC level.
PNGase F treatment confirmed glycosylation of RBD variants with manually added N-glycan sites (Y369N, A372T, L390S, G413N).
key_numbers: MW: 39 kDa (3 N-glycans), 35 kDa (2 N-glycans), 31 kDa (1 N-glycan), 27 kDa (deglycosylated)

canonical_events.summary: N-glycan mutants added to Delta and Omicron S proteins increased affinity (1.28–1.50-fold) and mAb resistance in vitro.
Pseudovirus infectivity drastically reduced for N-glycan mutants (e.g., L390S reduced infectivity ~816-fold) despite increased affinity.
Systematic pseudovirus testing identified eight mutations (S205F, Y453F, Q493A, Q493M, Q498H, Q498Y, N501F, N501T) with 3–12-fold increased infectivity over WT.
No linear correlation between affinity and infectivity; S2/S ratio (furin cleavage) correlated with infectivity.

Neutralization assay of high-risk mutants against commercial mAbs: Q493A/M resistant to LY-CoV555 (8–58-fold); Y453F, Q493A resistant to REGN10933 (48–606-fold); Q498Y/H resistant to REGN10987 and VIR-7831 (3–19-fold).
Serum neutralization assay: Q498Y exhibited broader defense against serum antibodies.

edges.reason: Problem of limited DMS studies motivates hypothesis of mammalian display.
Hypothesis leads to design of lentivirus surface-display system.
Design implemented, but initial WT-S cell line fails to bind ACE2.
Failure prompts redesign with furin cleavage site mutation and proline stabilization.
Stable Δfurin-S template enables library design for saturation mutagenesis.
Library design requires quality control to ensure high coverage.
High-coverage library used for transduction at low MOI.
Sequencing data analyzed to classify mutational effects and identify key residues.
Key residues validated by individual ACE2 binding assays.
Validation leads to investigation of N-glycosylation role in affinity.
Glycosylation confirmed, then tested in Delta and Omicron contexts.
Increased affinity in vitro contradicted by drastic loss of pseudovirus infectivity.
Systematic pseudovirus testing identifies high-infectivity mutants distinct from N-glycan mutants.
Infectivity data analyzed to reveal lack of correlation with affinity and role of furin cleavage.
High-risk mutants further tested for mAb resistance.
mAb resistance leads to serum neutralization testing.

must_cover_points: Failure of WT-S binding and redesign to Δfurin-S
Classification of mutational effects and key residues
Contradiction: N-glycan mutants increase affinity but reduce infectivity
Lack of correlation between affinity and infectivity; furin cleavage role
mAb and serum neutralization of high-risk mutants
